summary: Big news from the SE BeltLine today with Reynoldstown finally getting some movement on that stalled development near the trail, plus Lewis Barbecue opened at Ansley Mall bringing Texas smoke to our doorstep. Meanwhile, the airport chaos continues with ICE agents deployed but not really helping much.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Long-Stalled BeltLine Development Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After five years of sitting empty, that corner lot at Chester and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is moving ahead with 85 townhomes and condos about a block from the Eastside Trail. The site's been cleared since 2021, so neighbors are probably relieved to see something happening.
topStory.body:
Remember that corner at Chester and Fulton Terrace that's been an empty lot for what feels like forever? Well, construction equipment is finally rolling in. Embry Development Company bought the 2-acre site from national developer Toll Brothers two years ago, inheriting the permits for 85 residential units that'll mix townhomes with for-sale condos.

The project, now called Camber Crossing, sits about a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail and just north of Memorial Drive. It's prime real estate in Reynoldstown, where anything new gets snapped up fast. The original buildings were cleared back in 2021, leaving neighbors to wonder if anything would ever get built.

Embry Development's managing partner says they're focused on creating housing that fits the neighborhood's character while taking advantage of that primo BeltLine access. Given how hot the market is for anything walkable to the trail, these units will probably move quick once they're ready.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

The project, now called Camber Crossing, sits about a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail and just north of Memorial Drive. stories.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur Gets 'Missing Middle' Housing Village for Under $700K
stories.summary: Finally, some relatively affordable housing near Decatur. The Cottages at Midway is bringing 18 standalone houses around shared courtyards on Midway Road, with a soft launch starting next week. It's aimed at being more accessible than Decatur's typical $700K median, though they haven't released exact prices yet.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices hitting a median of over $700K, the new Cottages at Midway development is trying to offer something more accessible. The mixed-income community will have 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards and greenspace on 2.1 acres at 2883 and 2893 Midway Road.

The project is replacing two underused lots on a residential street in unincorporated Decatur, close to a major commercial corridor and downtown. The 'village-style' approach with shared spaces is part of the 'missing middle' housing concept that's been gaining traction around metro Atlanta. Construction is underway now, and developers say they'll start a soft launch next week to drum up interest.

While the exact pricing hasn't been announced, the goal is to create more accessible single-family ITP housing. Given Decatur's red-hot market, anything under the current median would be welcome relief for buyers who want to stay intown but can't stomach those price tags.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of Cottages at Midway development

stories.headline: West End's New Townhomes Along BeltLine Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: The West End is getting some rare for-sale townhomes right along the BeltLine. Ackerman & Co.'s GW Parkside project at 600 Hopkins Street is rising up, with 23 units planned and the first seven coming to market this spring. It's the same developer behind Lee + White, so they know the area.
stories.body:
If you've been walking the Southwest BeltLine near the West End, you've probably noticed construction happening at 600 Hopkins Street. That's GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes), and it's finally taking shape after a year of demo and prep work.

Ackerman & Co., the same folks behind the popular Lee + White development, is bringing 23 new townhomes to this stretch of the BeltLine corridor. The first seven properties are nearing completion and should hit the market this spring, according to onsite signage. For-sale townhomes along the BeltLine are pretty rare in Southwest Atlanta, so this could be a big deal for folks wanting to own rather than rent.

The West End has been heating up as more people discover its walkability to the BeltLine and downtown. With Ackerman's track record at Lee + White, they understand how to develop in this area without losing its character. Expect these units to move fast once they're ready.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Texas Smoke to the BeltLine at Ansley Mall
stories.summary: The Austin barbecue legend just opened behind The Cook's Warehouse at Ansley Mall, and their upstairs smokehouse with panoramic BeltLine views is unlike anything in Atlanta. Six 22-foot smokers run 24/7 to turn out what might be the city's best brisket. John Lewis himself helped weld those smokers from old propane tanks.
stories.body:
Lewis Barbecue just opened at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse, and honestly, their setup is wild. They put their smokehouse upstairs in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Six massive 22-foot smokers fill the space like some fancy engine room, running 24 hours a day in eight-hour shifts.

John Lewis, the pitmaster behind this operation, helped weld those smokers out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks. The guy helped put Austin barbecue on the map before expanding to Charleston, and now he's bringing that same obsessive attention to Atlanta. The brisket is getting rave reviews already, with people saying it might be the best you'll find in the city.

The location is perfect too. You can grab some of the best barbecue in town, then walk it off on the BeltLine. The panoramic windows let you watch the whole smoking operation while you eat, which is pretty cool. If you're serious about barbecue, this is going to be your new spot.

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Opens in Inman Park With Asian-American Comfort Food
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. The pop-up-turned-permanent spot serves everything from soft shell crab bao to Peking chicken platters, with Thai tea martinis to wash it down. Both chefs have serious credentials from Gordon Ramsay kitchens.
stories.body:
Mushi Ni officially opened today in Inman Park, marking a big milestone for chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez. The couple transformed their pop-up concept into a brick-and-mortar restaurant on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The location couldn't be better for grabbing food and heading to the trail.

Both chefs bring serious experience to the venture. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, while Le has deep roots in the local restaurant scene. Their menu focuses on Asian-American comfort food with dishes like soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa, and those Instagram-worthy crab Rangoon puffs.

The Peking chicken platter feeds two people for just $25, which is a steal for Inman Park. They're serving lunch and dinner with cocktails, including Thai tea martinis that sound perfect for sipping on a patio day. The Freedom Park Trail location means you can easily walk or bike over from the BeltLine corridor.
stories.imageAlt: Interior of Mushi Ni restaurant in Inman Park

stories.headline: Eastside Gets New Food Hall From NYC's Gansevoort Market Creator
stories.summary: Robert Montwaid, who created New York's famous Gansevoort Market, is opening his third Atlanta food hall at the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club. The six-concept food hall opens in mid-April with chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and outdoor dining. It's his first eastside location.
stories.body:
The eastside is getting a major food destination courtesy of Robert Montwaid, the guy who created New York City's legendary Gansevoort Market. Vela Park Food Works is finishing construction at the new rental community near East Lake Golf Club, with an opening slated for mid-April.

This will be Montwaid's third multi-concept food hall in Atlanta, but his first on the eastside. The space will feature six different chef-driven concepts along with craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining. The setup sounds similar to his other successful Atlanta projects, but tailored to the eastside crowd.

The location at the nearly 12-acre Vela Park community puts it right in the heart of some hot neighborhoods. East Lake has been growing as a dining destination, and this kind of chef-driven food hall could really put it on the map. With Montwaid's track record of picking great concepts, this should be worth the trip from anywhere ITP.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Avenue Bike Lanes Almost Done
stories.summary: That decade-long project to turn West Howard into a people-friendly Complete Street is finally wrapping up. The cycle track with concrete barriers runs from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle near East Lake MARTA, connecting Decatur High to the city limits. It's been a long time coming but looks like it'll be worth the wait.
stories.body:
Remember when West Howard Avenue was basically a drag strip for cars cutting through Decatur? Well, that's about to be history. The West Howard Avenue Cycle Track and Traffic Calming Improvements project is almost finished after starting construction last August, and the transformation is pretty dramatic.

The roughly one-mile stretch now features a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers running from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) to Paden Circle at the northern edge of East Lake MARTA station. New sidewalks, better infrastructure, and fresh pavement complete the makeover from car-focused road to people-friendly Complete Street.

This project has been in the works for nearly a decade, so folks in Decatur are probably relieved to see it finally happening. The concrete barriers for the bike lanes are a nice touch too. No more worrying about cars drifting into the cycling space. It's the kind of infrastructure that makes biking from MARTA to downtown Decatur actually feel safe.
stories.imageAlt: New cycle track on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deployed to Atlanta Airport But Mostly Just Standing Around
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents are now walking around Hartsfield-Jackson to help with the TSA chaos from the government shutdown, but travelers say they're not really doing much. The agents aren't handling security screenings or immigration enforcement, just providing 'security presence' while TSA agents work without pay.
stories.body:
ICE agents are now a common sight at Hartsfield-Jackson, deployed by the Trump administration to help deal with massive security lines caused by TSA staffing shortages during the partial government shutdown. But if you're flying through Atlanta, don't expect them to actually speed up your wait time.

The agents aren't involved with security screenings or immigration enforcement. They're basically just providing a 'security presence' while TSA employees continue working without pay. Travelers are reporting they see the agents walking around but not really helping with the bottlenecks that are causing four-hour waits.

Mayor Andre Dickens assured residents the agents won't be doing immigration enforcement during this deployment, but their actual role seems pretty unclear. With nearly 3,500 TSA agents calling out Monday (the highest rate since the shutdown began), it's going to take more than federal agents standing around to fix this mess. The real solution is ending the shutdown and paying these essential workers.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls as Fire Union Contract Fight Continues
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions barring ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and requiring documentation when ICE agents work with APD. The full council votes April 20. Meanwhile, dozens showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighter union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE activities in the city, setting up a full council vote on April 20. One would bar Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facilities within city limits, while the other requires Atlanta police to document whenever ICE agents are present during their operations.

The committee meeting drew dozens of residents who came to support the ICE resolutions, but many also used the opportunity to blast Mayor Andre Dickens for his refusal to sign the firefighter union contract. The council ratified the city's first-ever contract with the Atlanta firefighters' union back in April 2025, but Dickens has been sitting on it ever since.

The timing is pretty loaded, given that ICE agents are currently deployed at Hartsfield-Jackson during the government shutdown. These resolutions wouldn't affect that specific situation, but they're clearly meant to limit future ICE activities in Atlanta. The firefighter contract dispute shows how labor issues are becoming a bigger political headache for the mayor.

stories.headline: 15-Year-Old Arrested After Gun Found at Hillgrove High
stories.summary: A weapon detection system caught a gun in a student's possession at Hillgrove High School in Cobb County this morning, triggering a Code Red lockdown. The 15-year-old was arrested within 12 minutes and nobody was hurt, but it left parents and students pretty shaken up. The whole thing was resolved fast but felt much longer.
stories.body:
Hillgrove High School went into Code Red lockdown this morning after a weapon detection system flagged a firearm on a 15-year-old student. The school's response was swift, with officers locating the student, making an arrest, and recovering the weapon within about 12 minutes of the initial detection.

Parents got the scary notification and many rushed to the school, creating long lines outside the main entrance as administrators worked to resolve the situation. Students said the gun was reportedly found in the cafeteria. While nobody was injured and the response time was impressive, those 12 minutes felt much longer for families dealing with the uncertainty.

This kind of incident is exactly why Cobb County and other districts are investing heavily in weapon detection technology. The system worked as designed, catching the weapon before any harm could occur. But it's still a reminder of the reality our schools face every day, and why these security measures have become necessary in the first place.
stories.imageAlt: Police response at Hillgrove High School

stories.headline: 8-Foot Python Spotted on the BeltLine Sparks Safety Concerns
stories.summary: Someone found an 8- to 10-foot python on the Atlanta BeltLine and video shows a man handling it bare-handed, but wildlife experts are warning people not to touch unknown animals. The snake wasn't native to Georgia and officials don't know if it was someone's escaped pet or what. Definitely not your typical BeltLine wildlife sighting.
stories.body:
The Atlanta BeltLine has seen its share of urban wildlife, but an 8-foot python definitely wasn't on anyone's bingo card. Video captured by Joseph Ragland shows the massive snake in some bushes along the trail, and then a man yanking it out bare-handed to lay it across the path. Wildlife experts are not impressed with this approach.

Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue says the video is 'very concerning' because handling unknown snakes is dangerous for both the animal and the person. The python clearly wasn't a native Georgia species, so it was likely someone's escaped or abandoned pet. But there's no way to know if it was venomous or carrying diseases without proper expertise.

The incident highlights a growing problem with exotic pets in urban areas. When people can't handle their pythons or boa constrictors anymore, they sometimes just release them into the wild. That's bad for the ecosystem and potentially dangerous for trail users. If you see something like this on the BeltLine, call wildlife control instead of trying to be a hero.
